title: Install Stable Diffusion 3 Locally: Step-by-Step with StableSwarmUI & ComfyUI
description:
Welcome to another tutorial! In this video, I'll guide you through the process of installing Stable Diffusion 3 on your local machine using two different interfaces: StableSwarmUI and ComfyUI. Whether you're a beginner or an advanced user, this step-by-step guide will help you set up everything you need to start generating stunning AI images right away.

First, I'll show you how to install StableSwarmUI on Windows. We'll go through downloading the necessary files, setting up the folder structure, and running the installer. I'll also explain how to configure your settings and customize the interface to suit your preferences.

Next, we'll move on to ComfyUI. I'll provide detailed instructions on downloading, extracting, and setting up ComfyUI. You'll learn how to install custom nodes, manage workflows, and load Stable Diffusion models for the best results.

Join my Discord server for news, resources, and discussions about AI tools. Share your AI-generated images, and let's learn from eac
transcript: Welcome to another tutorial.  StableDefusion3 has just been released,  and today I'll show you how to install it  and start using it immediately on two interfaces.  The first interface is stableSWormUI,  and the second one is ComfyUI,  so you can choose the one you prefer.  Let's begin with the SwarmUI.  I'll provide the links in the description.  It's made by StabilityAI,  and you can find it on GitHub.  Currently, it's still in beta.  You can install it on various operating systems,  but since I have Windows,  I'll demonstrate how to install it on that.  If you're using Windows 10,  you'll need to manually install Git and .NET 8 first.  However, if you're using Windows 11,  this process is done automatically.  Click on the provided link to download a BAT file.  After downloading, create a new folder.  Remember not to place it in the program files folder.  Instead, choose a location like your D drive.  Create a new folder there and name it whatever you prefer.  Let's go with Swarm for now.  Once the folder is created,  move the downloaded BAT file into it.  Now, navigate to the folder you created earlier  and double-click to open it.  Windows might prevent it from running,  but you can click on More Info  and then select Run anyway.  If you're concerned about security,  you can scan the file for viruses online,  but rest assured, this file is safe.  Now it's downloading .NET version 8,  which is around 220 megabytes in size.  I'll fast forward the video during this download process  since it might take some time.  Remember not to close any Windows  until the download is complete.  An installer window will appear  and it will begin installing what was downloaded earlier.  Once the installation is complete,  it will proceed to download or update Git.  After that, Git will be installed on your computer  followed by some configuration steps.  Once everything is done,  you'll finally get the Swarm UI installer.  At this point, you'll encounter a legal notice.  The Swarm UI is under the MIT license,  but the models like stable diffusion three  are only for personal use.  For commercial use, you'll need to purchase a license.  Click on the Agreed button to proceed.  At this stage, you have the option  to simply click on Install,  but I'll choose to customize settings  to explore the available options  after that I'll click Next to proceed with the installation.  Here, you can choose a theme that suits your preference.  I think I'll go with Cyber, then I'll click Next.  Here, you'll find more options for advanced users,  but I'll stick with the default settings and click Next.  Moving on, you'll encounter options for the comfy UI.  I'll also go with the default comfy UI.  For those with an API key,  there's a second option available  and the last option is for custom back-end configurations.  Now, you'll reach the part where you can choose  which models to download.  Since all the available models are base models  that aren't very good,  I won't select any of them.  Instead, I'll download my own models separately.  Final step is to confirm by clicking on,  yes, I am sure, install now.  And once clicked, you'll need to wait  for the installation to complete.  And in the command window, you can monitor the progress  at the end of the process.  You'll be greeted with the interface.  Note that the top bar will disappear  after loading is complete.  Let's check the installation folder  to see what we have there.  And if you want to update the Swarm UI,  you can click on Update Windows  or the appropriate option for your operating system.  Similarly, if you want to launch the interface,  you would select Launch Windows.  Again, choose the appropriate option  for your operating system.  To close the existing Swarm UI,  simply close the command window.  Now, if you click on the launch.bat file,  it should successfully launch the interface.  What I usually do is right-click on the bat file  and create a shortcut.  Then I rename that shortcut to Swarm UI.  After that, I right-click on the shortcut,  select Properties and choose Change icon.  If it says there are no icons available, I click OK.  And then it presents me with some system icons.  From there, I can choose any icon I like,  such as a star, for example.  Click OK, then Apply, and finally OK again.  You can then copy and paste that shortcut  onto your desktop for easy access.  If you double-click on the shortcut,  you'll see that it runs the interface smoothly.  Now, let's get those stable diffusion three models.  You'll need to go to the Hugging Face website.  Links provided in the description.  Once there, you'll need to log in.  If you don't have an account, simply click on Register,  just like I'm doing now.  You'll be prompted to add your email and choose a password.  Click Next, then enter your username, full name,  check the I agree box, and finally create your account.  It asks me to confirm that I am human.  These captures are getting more interesting.  Then you check your email for confirmation  and click on the link you received.  Once you do that, you'll get a message confirming  that you are verified now on the download page.  You need to enter your name, email, country, and company.  You can also choose whether you want to receive email updates.  Make sure to check the box that says,  I acknowledge that the model is for non-commercial use  and then hit the Agree button.  On the Model Card tab, you can read more about the models.  However, you need to click on the Files and Versions tab  to actually get the models.  The smallest one available is the SD3 medium.  If you're using other interfaces, you can download  the second one.  But I will get the 4.34 gigabyte version  after downloading, you'll need to place it in the models folder  and then the stable diffusion folder.  Click Save and wait for it to download.  Next, we navigate to the Swarm UI interface  and click on the Models tab.  Here you'll find a Refresh button, which you can use  to ensure the interface shows the model you just downloaded.  You can select the model from the drop-down menu.  And in this case, it should say SD3 medium.  If you choose None, it will deselect that model.  Another method to load the model  is by clicking on the model thumbnail,  specifically on the image preview, not on the text.  Once clicked, you'll see that it's now loaded.  Let's see if it works.  And you can choose from resolution options,  a ratio or input, a custom ratio if you prefer,  and then type a prompt of what you want to see,  such as acute cartoon bunny and a forest.  After that, hit Generate.  Please note that only the first time you have to wait,  you'll notice that it downloads a file called ClipG  and another one called ClipL.  Once it's ready, it will generate the image based on your prompt.  Let's say it's OK for the first try.  These Clip models are texting coders  and can be found in the Texting coders folder.  And you can see that both models are here.  And it's nice that Swarm UI downloads them for you.  What it did was place them in the models folder  and inside the Clip folder.  You can change the seed from here.  If I click on Reuse, I get the seed of the prompt I just used.  If I click on DICE, it's random.  The nice thing is that every setting has a question mark.  So you can see what it does.  Let's see what settings are recommended for this model.  Messed up some settings in the recording,  but these are the settings you should use.  For steps, use 28 steps.  For CFG scale, use 4.5, then go to Sampling  and enable the Sampler.  Choose DPM plus plus 2M for the Sampler  and for the scheduler, select SGM Uniform.  Hit Generate to test it out.  You can enable this option with Texting Codings.  Here you can choose from different options,  depending on how good your video card is.  Keep in mind that this can take more of RAM  from what I understand.  I'll choose one option.  When I hit Generate, you'll see that the first time  it download another model called T5 in the Clip folder,  or you can choose both Clip and T5.  I'll have to play more with those options  in the next few days.  If you check the licenses on Stability AI,  you'll see that it's free for non-commercial use.  However, it costs $20 for commercial use.  So see which license works best for you.  Every time you hit Generate, the resulting image  is saved in the output folder, local raw,  and labeled with the date of creation.  You can create a shortcut to that folder for easy access.  You can download more models from Sivit AI website,  click on Models, then navigate to the filters  where you can sort them by popularity,  such as the most popular in the last month.  I personally like to use Safe Tensor Models.  Among them, the most popular ones are the SDXL and V1.5 Models.  One that I frequently use is Juggernaut XL,  and in this case, version 10.  The hyperversion is faster for those with lower VRAM,  but I prefer the results of the X version,  so I click Download and put it in the same folder  under Models and stable diffusion.  While it downloads, you can check the recommended settings  for each model, so you know what settings to use  when you utilize this model.  The one I downloaded is the Normal Model.  For Ratio, you can use a custom ratio  and input the exact width and height if you want,  but I will go with the default size in this case.  For Samplers, I use the DPM++,  and the Scheduler is Keras.  I'll also turn off the SD3 encoding.  For Steps, it says 3040, so I'll use 30 since it's a bit faster.  For the CFG, it's recommended between 3.7, so I'll use 5.  You can check the info on how the CFG influences the generation.  If your video card isn't so powerful,  you can try using a smaller model,  like an older version of Juggernaut,  such as the Reborn version 1.5 model. You can access Server Info,  where you can see CPU and video card usage.  On the Server configuration,  you can select your root folders.  Right now, it's set to the model root in the Models folder,  which means this folder from here.  Then you'll see all those folders  that you have inside with the name for each.  The models are now downloaded and we can test them.  If your interface is open,  you'll need to click Refresh to be able to see them.  You can see information about the type  and what resolution they are trained on.  Click on the Model thumbnail to load it.  I will test all three models using a fixed seed  with the settings already set for the Juggernaut XL model.  I'll add a prompt generated with Chat GPT  and then hit Generate.  The result is quite nice.  Next, let's test the smaller one, version 1.5,  which can handle smaller images.  The result looks like this.  Finally, for SD3,  I'll input the recommended settings for that model.  To get the best quality, I'll enable all I can.  The result is this.  And here is a side-by-side comparison.  Let's move on to the second part  where I show you how to install Comfy UI.  You'll find the link in the description.  Scroll down until you find Installing.  Since I have Windows,  I'll click on the direct link download.  Then in my D drive, I'll create a folder named Comfy.  You can give it any name you want.  Save that zip file inside that folder.  Go to that folder and wait for it to finish downloading.  Then extract the contents of that archive.  It might take a while.  Once extracted, navigate inside the new folder.  You can read the ReadMe file for any instructions.  Assuming you have an NVIDIA GPU,  click on the run NVIDIA GPU.bat file.  A command window will open  where you can see some settings  and information about your video card.  Then Comfy UI will be ready.  And this is how the interface looks like.  I also recommend getting the Comfy UI manager  from this page.  You can scroll down to the installation section  and you'll see it has three steps.  I'll copy the text from step number two  and then I'll start with the first step.  Go to the Comfy UI folder and navigate  to the custom nodes folder.  Then in the address bar, type cmd and press enter.  This will open a command window in that exact folder.  Here you paste the text and press enter.  This will use Git to clone that manager into your folder.  Once it's ready, you can close that window.  Let's go back to the folder where we installed Comfy UI.
